TDEE Lab
Calculator What is TDEE BMR vs TDEE

BMR vs TDEE

Two numbers every person interested in nutrition should understand: BMR (Basal Metabolic Rate) and TDEE (Total Daily Energy Expenditure). They're related but serve very different purposes.

BMR

Basal Metabolic Rate

The calories your body burns at complete rest to keep you alive — breathing, circulation, cell repair. Think of it as your body's "minimum operating cost."

≈ 1,500 – 2,200 kcal/day

TDEE

Total Daily Energy Expenditure

The total calories your body burns in a full day — including BMR, exercise, walking, digesting food, and all non-exercise activity.

≈ 1,800 – 3,500 kcal/day

The Relationship

TDEE = BMR × Activity Multiplier

Your TDEE is always higher than your BMR (unless you're completely sedentary and even then, TEF adds to it). The activity multiplier bridges the gap.

Which One Should You Use?

  • Use BMR to understand your baseline metabolic health and compare against clinical standards.
  • Use TDEE to set your actual calorie targets for weight loss, maintenance, or gain. This is the number that matters for nutrition planning.

How Activity Affects the Gap

The difference between BMR and TDEE can be dramatic. A sedentary office worker might have a TDEE only 20% above their BMR, while a competitive athlete could have a TDEE 90% above their BMR.

Activity Multiplier TDEE (BMR=1,700)
Sedentary1.2×2,040 kcal
Light1.375×2,338 kcal
Moderate1.55×2,635 kcal
Active1.725×2,933 kcal
Very Active1.9×3,230 kcal

Key Takeaway

BMR tells you what your body needs to survive. TDEE tells you what your body needs to function at your actual activity level. For any practical nutrition goal — whether losing fat, building muscle, or maintaining weight — TDEE is the number that matters.
